topic: SAA Exponential Cell Separation — c7 change-driven review of post-c6 tldr_verdict expansion (cross-reference link insertion)
change_summary: tldr_verdict_length grew from 165 → 368 chars (+123.03%) since c6 review (2026-05-09T08:22:21Z); sourced from drift-audit.json generated 2026-05-28T01:09:01Z (age ~30h, fresh). obe_skipped=1 (WIN-006 already re-reviewed since audit — latest WIN-006.c6.json 2026-05-28T04:18:38Z post-dates audit last_review 2026-05-18T18:44:41Z). The drift originates from commit e8a6affb (2026-05-11T13:25:59Z, 'Decider self-apply: EXP-306 patches + ISS-1963/1967 + M1 sweep + SEC-6.14 c2 triage'): the decider extended the WIN-004 tldr_verdict from a flat closing sentence to one carrying two new HTML cross-reference links — `Part 3.1b post-hoc test` and `timestamp-anchoring error`. The change was part of an EXP-306 patch batch (M1 verification batch 5). All other fingerprint fields (claim, finding, detail_evidence, detail_verdict_text, detail_extra=null, tldr_evidence) are unchanged since c6.
our_argument_summary: Std Model Explains. The c6 review summary stands: MHD via Terra-Nova et al. (2017) provides the spatial mechanism for SAA bifurcation; westward drift ~0.792 °/yr is core-flow advection; African–American decay asymmetry is LLSVP-modulated; dome H(r) = 8537·exp(−r/8619) km is purely radial and cannot produce azimuthal asymmetry. Bifurcation rate (30.8° → 50.6° over 25 years) is observational on both sides; the verdict is backed by the post-hoc trap. c7 reviews a tldr_verdict cross-reference expansion (e8a6affb, 2026-05-11) that added two `<a>` links pointing readers to the post-hoc-test self-test panel and the timestamp-anchoring-error tab. The intent (cross-link the methodological objection from the parenthetical of the long verdict text into the TLDR) is sound, but the build pipeline escapes HTML in tldr fields, so the links render as literal HTML markup to readers. This is the primary new hole. Defense rating drops from c6=2 to c7=3 because the TLDR now visually presents as broken markup, which is a credibility hit independent of the science.

relevant_files: raw-text/05-model.txt line 14-16 (H(r) function as pure radial)
raw-text/08-live-power.txt (live-fetch list — SAA decay rate, not separation)
dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: Re-verified in c7 clone. 50.57° remains hardcoded in 05-model.txt:43. The live-fetch list at 08-live-power.txt still tracks decay rate (WIN-037/042 family) and not the inter-cell angular separation that WIN-004 cites. PRED-013's 30-year-horizon 120–180° band (raw-text/03-predictions.txt:45) remains a manual long-horizon claim, not auto-monitored. H(r) = 8537·exp(−r/8619) km (raw-text/05-model.txt:16) — re-verified pure radial. The H(r) finding remains the structural fingerprint of the dome's inability to produce azimuthal asymmetry, faithful to the dome's own published model.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: Unchanged from c6: 50.57° remains the static architectural constant; two-point endpoints underdetermine the curve family; τ remains unpublished by the dome. No new derivation appeared in the c6→c7 interval.
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: Unchanged: the unpublished τ is still the rhetorical escape hatch. c6→c7 made no dome-side change that alters this.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: None new. The c3-era live_fetch / hardcoded resolution holds. The c5/c6 patch landings remain intact.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: Re-verified in c7 clone at run start. raw-text/05-model.txt:43 'SAA separation (2025): 50.57 deg' is a static architectural constant; the 20-domain live-fetch suite tracks SAA decay rate (nT/yr) at INTERMAGNET stations, which feeds WIN-037/042 family, not WIN-004's inter-cell angular separation. The WIN-004/005 cluster shares the hardcoded tag — cross-WIN consistent with the c6 finding. The c7 e8a6affb commit added link references in the TLDR but did not introduce any new monitor.py path; tag is unchanged.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: Terra-Nova et al. (2017) PEPI 266:39-53 (DOI 10.1016/j.pepi.2017.03.002) remains the load-bearing globe-side citation: it derives SAA bifurcation from reversed-flux patches and core flow, predates the dome's prediction commits, and the dome relabels the same mechanism 'aetheric vortex decay' without altering the predicted value. c5→c6 patches reinforced the LLSVP attribution in the verdict synthesis; c7's link addition does not change the substance.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: CHAOS-7 (Finlay et al. 2020), INTERMAGNET, ESA Swarm L1B, IGRF-14 are all publicly indexed and predate the dome's monitor commit. The dome author's own definition ('prediction was timestamped before confirming data was pulled') is inapplicable because the monitor hardcodes the target on the same commit as the data-fetch line. The c7 TLDR cross-reference to #timestamp-error reinforces this point at the reader level — when the rendering bug is fixed (see holes_found), the cross-link will helpfully drive readers from the TLDR to the dedicated Timestamp Error tab where the OTS anchoring critique is fully developed.
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: Re-verified: PROS-001 (raw-text/02-wins.txt:14-15) publishes 'exponential separation curve' as a label without τ. WIN-004 (raw-text/02-wins.txt:62-63) confirms with no derivation. The c5 patch text 'the exponential rate parameter τ is unpublished' remains in detail_verdict_text. No derivation path from disc radius / H(r) / aether coupling κ to the 50.6° value exists in any raw-text file. The c7 TLDR change did not introduce a new derivation claim.

holes_found.description: TLDR HTML markup renders as escaped text, not as functional links. The c6→c7 tldr_verdict expansion (commit e8a6affb, 2026-05-11) added two `<a>` cross-reference links to data/wins.json:WIN-004.tldr_verdict. But build-scripts/generate-html.js wraps tldr_verdict in escapeHtml() at line 840 (and tldr_evidence at line 828) before injection, so the user-visible output in docs/index.html is literal markup: `<a href="#posthoc-standard" onclick="showTab("selftest");return false">Part 3.1b post-hoc test</a>`. Readers see the entire HTML string — angle brackets, quoted attributes, the onclick handler, all of it — as plaintext inside the TLDR paragraph. The TLDR is the first thing a reader sees before expanding the verdict panel, so this is a high-visibility defect. The cross-WIN consistency scan (see cross_win_consistency below) confirms the same bug fires on WIN-052 (escaped `<sub>` tag in λ<sub>A</sub>), WIN-053 (escaped `<a>` link to §6.1), and WIN-064 (escaped `<em>` tag in 'every' emphasis) — at minimum 4 WINs ship with broken TLDR markup. The decider's intent on each was sound (cross-link, subscript, emphasis), but the rendering pipeline silently discards it. Compounding: the c6 review (this curmudgeon) did not catch this hole because the c6 fingerprint comparison detected only c5→c6 changes (which were detail_verdict_text/finding additions, both of which render correctly because those fields are NOT escapeHtml'd at lines 832 and 853-857), and c5→c6 happened to leave tldr_verdict at 165 chars unchanged — the TLDR-with-links change came in c6→c7 and was not visible to the c6 reviewer.
holes_found.recommendation:
Two viable paths. (a) Per-WIN fix: strip HTML markup from WIN-004/052/053/064's tldr fields and substitute plain-text references — e.g., for WIN-004 tldr_verdict closing parenthetical, replace the two `` links with 'see Part 3.1b post-hoc test in the Self-Test tab and the dedicated Timestamp Error tab.' This is a 4-WIN, ~100-char edit, low risk. (b) Systemic fix: change generate-html.js:828 and :840 to skip escapeHtml() for tldr_evidence/tldr_verdict and instead pass through a curated sanitizer (allow-list: ``, ``, ``, ``, ``, `
`; deny everything else). This unlocks the link/emphasis pattern that the decider clearly wants to use for cross-references. Option (b) is the better architectural fix because the link pattern is high-leverage (cross-references inside TLDRs drive readers to the Self-Test and Timestamp-Error tabs that are the review's two strongest methodological moves); option (a) is the quick patch that closes the visible bug without architectural change. The author of this curmudgeon review recommends (b), with a tests/run.sh assertion that renders WIN-004's tldr_verdict and grep-checks for `<a ` to prevent regression.

holes_found.description: Carry-over from c6 hole #1 (formerly c4/c5 hole #2): detail_evidence still contains the unsourced phrase 'is consistent with westward-drift advection in broader MHD models' (re-verified at data/wins.json:WIN-004.detail_evidence). This is now its FOURTH consecutive stagnation cycle (c4→c5→c6→c7 unaddressed). The c5 recommendation offered the option-(b) softening ('westward-drift advection regime modeled by core-flow MHD' — single-phrase change) but no patch landed in the c6→c7 interval. Severity remains minor because the verdict rests on the structural mechanism gap, not on a rate-prediction citation — but the citation hygiene gap has now accumulated four cycles of rhetorical cost. The c6 review noted this stagnation; the c7 review confirms no progress and flags the pattern.
holes_found.recommendation: Adopt the c5 option-(b) softening as the minimum-friction close: replace 'is consistent with westward-drift advection in broader MHD models' with 'is consistent with the westward-drift advection regime modeled by core-flow MHD.' Single-phrase substitution, zero downstream risk. If the open-issues tracker still has no item for this, the decider should either (i) create one to drive the patch in the next self-apply cycle, or (ii) explicitly mark the recommendation wontfix and remove it from the review tracking surface so future cycles stop flagging it.

holes_found.description: Carry-over from c6 hole #2: detail_extra remains null (re-verified at data/wins.json:WIN-004.detail_extra). The PRED-013 feasibility math (linear 2055 extrapolation = 74.4°; lower-bound 120° requires 2.92× current rate; upper-bound 180° requires 5.45×) has now survived four patch cycles (c3→c4→c5→c6→c7) without integration. The math itself is preserved in c4/c5/c6/c7 review records, so any analyst regenerating prose can find it — but it does not surface to the reader. Severity remains minor because PRED-013 is a 30-year forecast and the long-horizon framing is already partially carried by the published 'exponential' label commentary. The c6 review noted this stagnation; c7 confirms.
holes_found.recommendation: Either (a) integrate the PRED-013 math into detail_extra as a 3-line block ('Linear extrapolation: 74.4° in 2055. PRED-013 lower bound: 2.92× current rate required. Upper bound: 5.45× current rate required.'), or (b) explicitly mark detail_extra as intentionally null for this WIN and remove the standing recommendation from review tracking. Decision is structural (do we use detail_extra at all for SAA WINs?) rather than per-WIN — flag for decider strategy call, not for another sentence-level patch.

stronger_arguments.description: The c6→c7 TLDR cross-reference pattern, ONCE THE RENDERING BUG IS FIXED, is high-leverage. Linking the TLDR closing parenthetical to (a) the dedicated post-hoc test panel in Part 3.1b and (b) the standalone Timestamp Error tab lets a reader who scans only the TLDR still discover the review's two strongest methodological moves. This is a better placement than burying the same links in the detail_verdict_text (where they currently live and render correctly, but only for readers who expand the panel). Recommend extending the pattern to all WINs whose verdict relies materially on the post-hoc test or the OTS anchoring critique — once the rendering bug is closed.

stronger_arguments.description: Aubert (2015) GJI 203:1738-1751 (DOI 10.1093/gji/ggv394) remains the proposed substitute citation for detail_evidence c5/c6/c7 hole — STILL NOT RETRIEVED. Recommend assigning to analyst as a small-scope research task. If the paper publishes a prospective rate-style prediction or a derivation of the 0.79°/yr separation rate, it converts the hole from 'citation hygiene' to 'MHD predicted, dome adopted' (substantive strengthening of the post-hoc trap). If it does not, falling back to the c5 option-(b) softening closes the hole at minimum cost.

stronger_arguments.description:
Cross-WIN reinforcement: the broken-TLDR-HTML bug surfaces a systemic build-pipeline issue. If the fix path is (b) systemic, the decider should also extend the per-WIN test surface — tests/run.sh should grep docs/index.html for '<a href=' inside `

` and fail if any are found. This converts the bug from a one-time catch into a regression guard. Out of scope for this c7 review file (a curmudgeon recommendation, not a curmudgeon implementation), but flagged for the decider's PROP queue.

kernel_of_truth.description: The c6→c7 tldr_verdict edit reflects good editorial instinct (data/wins.json:WIN-004.tldr_verdict). The decider EXP-306 batch identified that the WIN-004 TLDR — which a reader sees BEFORE expanding the verdict panel — closed with a flat sentence ('The data was publicly available before the prediction.') that gave the reader no path to the methodological infrastructure that backs the post-hoc claim. The c6→c7 edit replaces that flat closer with two cross-references that, in principle, route the reader to the Self-Test tab (where Part 3.1b enumerates the post-hoc-test methodology) and the Timestamp Error tab (where the OTS canary error is documented in detail). Both anchors exist and resolve correctly in docs/index.html (`id='posthoc-standard'` and `id='timestamp-error'`, re-verified). The editorial intent is exactly right — TLDR readers are the largest reader segment and most-vulnerable to dismissal, and routing them to the methodological infrastructure is a high-leverage move.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: But the execution is broken. build-scripts/generate-html.js:840 escapes the HTML markup, so the cross-references render as literal text in docs/index.html (verified at index 615893 in the rendered output: the link tags appear as `<a href="...">...</a>`, not as functional `<a>` elements). The reader sees raw markup instead of clickable links. This is worse than the pre-c7 state because (a) the pre-c7 flat sentence at least read cleanly, and (b) the post-c7 markup-as-text actively damages credibility — a reader who sees broken HTML in a TLDR may downgrade the entire review's editorial polish. The verdict 'Std Model Explains' continues to hold (data/wins.json:WIN-004.verdict) because the scientific argument is unchanged, but the TLDR presentation has regressed and needs the patch enumerated in holes_found item #1.

advocate_mode.best_defense: A fair-minded dome defender's strongest c7-era attack pivots on the TLDR rendering bug AND a frame-mixing critique. (1) 'Your debunk of WIN-004 has now broken its own TLDR. The closing parenthetical of your TLDR ends with literal `<a href=` markup visible to every reader who hovers over the verdict panel. This isn't a substantive critique of my model — it's evidence that your review's editorial layer is regressing. If you can't keep your own TLDR clean, why should I trust your characterization of the H(r) function as radially symmetric?' This is rebuttable on the science (the HTML bug is independent of the MHD argument), but the rebuttal requires the reader to grant the dome defender's invitation to separate science from presentation — exactly the move our review's six-principle framework asks the defender NOT to make in the other direction (toward us). The asymmetry hurts. (2) 'Even putting aside the broken HTML, your new TLDR mixes a scientific verdict ('Standard MHD simulations reproduce SAA splitting from core dynamics') with a methodological verdict ('The data was publicly available before the prediction'). The detail_verdict_text carefully separates these via the explicit hinge 'Independent of the scientific critique above, this claim also fails our post-hoc test.' The TLDR strings them together with a parenthetical, which signals to the casual reader that the methodological objection is part of the science. If a reader concludes your debunk hinges on a timestamping technicality, they may dismiss your MHD argument as secondary. Restore the separation in the TLDR.' This is partially valid — the c6/c7 TLDR does compress the two-pronged structure, and the cross-reference pattern (if it rendered correctly) would partly mitigate it by giving the reader two clearly-labeled link targets. (3) 'You acknowledge the bifurcation rate is observational on both sides. The new TLDR doesn't carry that concession forward. A reader of just the TLDR comes away thinking your MHD position predicts the rate; it doesn't. Be explicit about what is and isn't a prediction.' This is the c5/c6 unfinished business — the detail text concedes this; the TLDR does not. With only 368 chars to work with, the trade-off is real, but a sentence-level addition ('Bifurcation rate is observational on both sides; the verdict rests on the structural mechanism gap.') would close the attack at minimum cost.
advocate_mode.preemptive_recommendation: Three coordinated edits. (A) Fix the TLDR rendering bug per holes_found item #1 recommendation (b) systemic, or (a) per-WIN — this closes attack #1 entirely. (B) Restore the c6 frame separation in the TLDR: rewrite tldr_verdict closing to read 'Bifurcation rate is observational on both sides; the verdict rests on the structural mechanism gap. The data was also publicly available before the prediction — see [Part 3.1b post-hoc test] and [Timestamp Error tab].' This closes attacks #2 and #3 simultaneously. (C) After (A) and (B), defense_survives should drop back to 2 or lower. The c6→c7 cross-reference move is salvageable and high-leverage; it just needs the rendering bug closed AND the frame separation restored.

cross_win_consistency.inconsistencies_found: On the scientific argument: no new inconsistencies in the c6→c7 interval — WIN-004/005 cluster still shares the hardcoded/relabel/post-hoc tag pattern, WIN-040 H(r) constraint cross-link is still future work. On the build-pipeline / TLDR rendering bug: WIN-004 (tldr_verdict, 2 broken `<a>` links), WIN-052 (tldr_verdict, 1 broken `<sub>` tag inside λ<sub>A</sub>), WIN-053 (tldr_verdict, 1 broken `<a>` link to §6.1), and WIN-064 (tldr_evidence, 1 broken `<em>` tag wrapping the word 'every') all suffer the same escapeHtml-stripping bug. The c7 review confirms WIN-004 is not an isolated incident — the bug is systemic across at least 4 WINs and the patch surface should be coordinated. WIN-052/053/064 should be added to the next priority queue push if the systemic fix is not adopted.

quantitative_verification.computations: Ran arithmetic via python3 inside the c7 clone (see preceding bash invocations in the curmudgeon agent log). All c5/c6-era arithmetic re-verified — no new numeric claims were added in c6→c7. The new claim is the 123.03% drift figure which matches the audit's pre-computed value exactly. The HTML-rendering verification used a substring search at the known WIN-004 tldr_verdict anchor in docs/index.html and counted escaped vs functional `<a>` tags in the surrounding 700-char region.

deeper_analysis:
This is a change-driven review triggered by tldr_verdict_length drift (165 → 368 chars, +123.03%) since c6 (2026-05-09T08:22:21Z). The drift originates from a single commit (e8a6affb, 2026-05-11) and reflects a deliberate editorial move by the decider during the EXP-306 M1 verification batch: replace the flat closing sentence of the TLDR with two HTML cross-reference links pointing to the Self-Test tab (Part 3.1b post-hoc test) and the Timestamp Error tab. The intent is sound and aligns with the review's broader strategy of cross-linking methodological infrastructure into high-traffic surfaces. The execution is broken: build-scripts/generate-html.js:840 wraps tldr_verdict in escapeHtml(), so the markup renders as literal text in docs/index.html. A cross-WIN consistency scan finds at least 3 other WINs (052, 053, 064) shipping with the same build-pipeline bug, indicating the issue is systemic and the patch surface should be coordinated rather than per-WIN. The carry-over holes from c4/c5/c6 (detail_evidence 'broader MHD models' phrase, detail_extra null) are now in their fourth consecutive stagnation cycle without any open-issues entry to track them — flagged for the decider to either spawn an issue or explicitly wontfix and remove from review tracking. The verdict 'Std Model Explains' continues to hold; advocate defense_survives rises from c6=2 to c7=3 because the broken-HTML rendering bug creates a new presentation-credibility attack surface independent of the science.

The-other-side check: is the c6→c7 TLDR rewrite actually an editorial regression? A charitable reading would say 'no — the pre-c7 TLDR was already underdeveloped, and once the rendering bug is fixed the c7 cross-reference pattern is strictly better.' I agree with that reading. The hole is in the rendering pipeline, not in the editorial decision. The c7 review's recommended_action is minor_edit (not major_rewrite) precisely because the new content is correct in intent and only needs one of two well-defined patches (per-WIN HTML strip or systemic sanitizer swap) to become functional. A second charitable angle: 'the build pipeline escape was intentional, defending against accidental HTML injection from analyst/curmudgeon outputs.' Plausible but unfounded — the same generate-html.js renders detail_evidence and detail_verdict_text without escapeHtml (lines 832 and 853-857), accepting the same HTML-injection risk in much-larger surface areas. The selective escape on tldr fields is more likely an oversight from when the TLDR fields were added (commit fcd38da5, 2026-04-12) than a security posture.

This is not a citation failure in the academic sense (the anchor targets exist), but it is a rendering failure that makes the citations inaccessible to readers. See holes_found item #1.
